name: Verdict — the AI Decision Log for regulated teams
pitch: Verdict watches every consequential yes/no decision your AI assistants make, runs it past a small panel of independent reviewers before it happens, blocks the dangerous ones, and gives you a tamper-evident logbook proving who (or what) approved each one — so when an auditor or your boss asks "why did the AI do that?", you have the receipt.
buyer: Who pays: a compliance lead, COO, or head of operations at a 50–500-person company (financial services, healthcare ops, professional services) that has started letting AI agents take actions — drafting filings, approving expenses, sending client comms, changing records. What triggers the purchase: the first scary near-miss (an AI agent almost did something irreversible), an upcoming audit or SOC 2 / regulatory exam, or a board/insurer asking "what's your AI governance policy?" and the honest answer being "we don't have one." What they pay: $1,500–4,000/month per team for the hosted service (tiered by decision volume and seats), or a $25–50k fixed-fee "AI Decision Governance" consulting engagement to install and tune it — Matt's existing engagement price point.
offering: Concretely they get: (1) a hosted "decision gateway" their AI tools call before acting — it convenes a configurable panel of independent AI reviewer "seats" (verified working today as Forseti/Mímir/Heimdall in thing-decide.py), each returning a verdict + confidence + injection-detection flag, and returns approve / block / send-to-a-human; (2) a safety envelope that ALWAYS routes irreversible/high-stakes actions to a named human (never auto-approved) — this is the trust anchor; (3) an append-only, tamper-evident Decision Log (the Sága log, already on disk as timestamped JSON records) with per-decision reasoning, who decided, and confidence; (4) a plain-English web dashboard showing every decision, a "needs your sign-off" queue, and reliability trends (which reviewer concerns turn out to be false alarms — already computed by thing-concern-stats.py); (5) an exportable audit pack (PDF/CSV) they hand to an auditor. A "policy posture" dial (off / advisory / binding, lifted from comfort-posture.yaml) lets a non-technical owner set how strict the gate is per category in one screen.
why_us: This is the single asset almost no one else has built and we have running in production on this repo: thing-decide.py is a real, self-contained multi-seat decision tribunal that today adjudicates live yes/no calls, detects prompt-injection per seat, defers on splits/low-confidence/high-blast, and writes audited JSON verdicts (real records exist under .ravenclaude/runs/thing/decisions/ dated 2026-06-04). The conservative defer-to-human safety envelope is already coded and documented. The Sága log + thing-concern-stats.py give us reliability analytics (stripped false-positives, heimdall-disagreed signals) that turn governance from a checkbox into a measurable system. The comfort-posture engine gives a non-developer the off/advisory/binding dial. And the Capability Grounding Protocol (the "name the actual error, cite the this-session check, never falsely claim can't/can" discipline) is exactly the credibility story a governance buyer needs. Competitors sell either model-side guardrails (content filters) or after-the-fact observability dashboards; we sell a pre-action, human-in-the-loop, audited approval gate — the missing middle.
simplicity_packaging: What the complex machinery hides: panel orchestration, per-seat model dispatch, injection scrubbing, split/abstain logic, Sága log integrity, concern-reliability stats. What the buyer SEES in the first 5 minutes: a one-screen setup that asks three questions — "Which AI tools should report to Verdict?" "How strict? (Watch only / Warn / Block)" "Who signs off on the risky ones?" — then a live dashboard with three things: a green/amber/red feed of recent decisions in plain English, a "2 decisions need your sign-off" inbox, and a big "Download audit report" button. No YAML, no code, no prompt engineering. The reviewer panel, confidence math, and injection defenses are invisible; the buyer just sees "3 independent reviewers checked this; 2 approved, 1 flagged — sent to you." The single most important visible promise: "Verdict will never let an AI do something it can't undo without a human saying yes first."
business_model: Consulting-first, productize-second — matches the documented RavenClaude strategy (do engagements, watch what gets reused, then SaaS). Phase 1: sell "AI Decision Governance" as a $25–50k fixed-fee engagement (install Verdict against the client's AI tools, define their decision categories and human-signoff routing, hand over the audit-pack process). 4–6/year solo is already the target capacity. Phase 2 (only after 3+ engagements prove which decision categories recur): hosted SaaS at $1,500–4,000/mo per team, plus a metered tier on decision volume. The audit-pack export and the reliability-trend dashboard are the natural upsell/retention hook (recurring exam/board cadence). The public ravenclaude-core stays the proof-of-craft front door; Verdict is the first private-IP pattern earning its way out into a product.
twelve_month_path: Months 1–2: extract thing-decide.py + the Sága log + concern-stats into a standalone hosted service behind a stable HTTP "decision gateway" API and a thin web app (reuse serve-dashboards.py endpoints + the existing dashboard generator). Months 2–3: land the first design partner from Matt's existing consulting pipeline as a paid engagement (governance over their AI-assisted finance/ops workflow) — this funds the build and produces the first real audit pack. Months 4–6: harden multi-tenant isolation (the data-platform plugin's RLS/JWT-embed skills already exist), add SSO, ship the off/advisory/binding posture UI and the audit-pack PDF export. Months 6–9: two more paid engagements; instrument which decision categories recur to define the SaaS default policy templates (e.g. "financial-services starter pack"). Months 9–12: open a self-serve hosted tier for the recurring pattern, priced $1,500–4,000/mo, seeded from the engagement clients converting to subscription. Gate at each step: only build the SaaS surface for a category after a real engagement has paid for and validated it.
honest_risks: The reviewer panel is itself AI — a sophisticated buyer (or regulator) will rightly ask 'who watches the watchers?' The honest answer is the human safety envelope (irreversible actions always defer to a person) and the audit log, not the AI panel's infallibility. If we oversell the panel as 'safe,' one bad auto-approval destroys the trust the whole product sells.

Running multiple model seats per decision costs real tokens and adds latency; for high-volume decision streams the unit economics could invert. Mitigation (already partly built): tiered panels (cheaper Haiku seats for low-stakes, escalate to Opus on splits) and the dispatch-evaluator/adaptive-classifier right-sizers — but this needs hard cost modeling before promising a per-decision price.

The current engine is single-tenant, file-on-disk, and dogfooded on one repo. 'It works for us' is not 'it survives a hostile multi-tenant SaaS with someone else's data.' Multi-tenant isolation, integrity guarantees on the log (true tamper-evidence, not just append-only files), and SSO are real engineering, not a weekend.

Governance/compliance is a slow, trust-heavy, reference-driven sale with long cycles — hard for a solo operator. The consulting-first path mitigates cash-flow risk, but scaling past Matt's personal bandwidth needs either a partner or a self-serve motion that the buyer profile (cautious compliance leads) may resist.

Legal exposure: positioning Verdict as making decisions 'compliant' or 'auditable' could imply a warranty we can't back. Must be framed as a control-and-evidence tool the customer's own humans operate, with clear 'not legal/financial advice' boundaries, or it invites liability.

Bigger players (model vendors shipping native guardrails, observability incumbents adding approval gates) could commoditize the category. Our defensible edge is the specific pre-action human-in-the-loop + audited-defer design and the Microsoft-stack consulting wedge — not the idea itself, which is copyable.

name: Aegis — The AI Governance Binder
pitch: When your insurer, your biggest customer, or your board asks "what are your AI tools actually doing, and how do you keep them safe?", Aegis turns the question into a one-click, evidence-backed PDF binder you can hand over the same day.
buyer: A 20–200-person SMB owner, COO, or ops/compliance lead (not a developer) who has started letting AI agents touch real work — drafting, data pulls, code, customer comms. The purchase is triggered by an external demand with a deadline and a dollar consequence: a cyber-insurance renewal questionnaire that now has an "AI/automation governance" section, a vendor security questionnaire (SIG/CAIQ-style) from a large customer that is gating a contract, or a board/auditor asking for an "AI usage and controls" memo. They pay because saying "we don't track that" risks a denied claim, a lost deal, or a failed audit. Pricing: 1,499/yr per company self-serve for the binder + live dashboard (Aegis Standard); 6,000–12,000 one-time "Attested Binder" engagement where RavenClaude (Matt) reviews the evidence and signs a reviewer attestation; 25–40k/yr for multi-entity firms needing per-entity binders.
offering: A living "AI Governance Binder" plus the live dashboard behind it. Concretely the buyer gets: (1) a branded PDF binder answering the standard questions every questionnaire asks — which AI agents run, what they're allowed/forbidden to touch, who approves risky actions, what happened when something was blocked, and how decisions are logged; (2) a pre-filled questionnaire pack — the same evidence mapped onto the common insurer + vendor-security + NIST-AI-RMF/ISO-42001 question sets, so they paste answers instead of inventing them; (3) a live read-only governance dashboard (the existing RavenClaude dashboard pipeline) showing the active guardrail posture, the running tally of agent decisions and what the tribunal allowed/denied/deferred, and the blocked-destructive-action log; (4) an evidence export — the tamper-evident Sága decision records and guard-hook logs zipped with a manifest, so an auditor can spot-check. Optional human attestation: Matt reviews the binder and adds a signed reviewer note (the upsell).
why_us: This is assembly of already-built, verified RavenClaude machinery, not a greenfield bet. (a) The Sága decision trail already exists and already records exactly the binder's core evidence — I read a real record at /workspaces/RavenClaude/.ravenclaude/runs/thing/decisions/decide-2026-06-04T19-52-46Z-811931.json: it carries the question, context, high_blast flag, mode, per-seat verdicts/confidence, an injection_detected flag, and a timestamp. That is a contemporaneous "what did the AI decide and why" audit row. (b) The guardrails are real PreToolUse hooks at plugins/ravenclaude-core/hooks/ — guard-destructive.sh, guard-web-access.sh, guard-recursive-spawn.sh, route-decision-review.sh — so "we physically block dangerous agent actions" is demonstrable, not aspirational. (c) The comfort-posture layer (.ravenclaude/comfort-posture.yaml, schema v5, set-posture skill) is the human-readable "what we allow/ask/deny" policy the binder quotes verbatim. (d) The regulatory-compliance plugin already has the control-testing, risk-register-build, and examination-readiness skills plus a risk-and-controls-specialist agent that speaks three-lines-of-defense and control-mapping — the exact framing insurers/auditors expect. (e) The dashboard generator pipeline already turns on-disk run data into a branded HTML surface. No competitor can credibly say "our governance binder is generated from our own agents' real tamper-evident logs" because almost no one has been dogfooding an agent-governance substrate for months.
simplicity_packaging: The buyer never sees a hook, a YAML file, or a JSON log. First five minutes: they open one page, answer three plain-language questions with dropdowns — "Where do your AI tools run?" (Microsoft 365 / Google / our laptops / a vendor app), "What's the riskiest thing they're allowed to do?", "Who needs the answers — an insurer, a customer, or our board?" — then click "Generate my binder." Out comes a finished PDF and a dashboard link. All the machinery (decision logs, guard hooks, posture YAML, control mapping) runs underneath and is surfaced only as three readable sections: "What our AI is allowed to do," "What we blocked," and "How every decision was logged." The questionnaire pack is literally copy-paste: each common question shows the pre-filled answer and a "source" link into the evidence. The complexity is hidden behind one verb the buyer already understands: hand it over.
business_model: Three tiers. (1) Aegis Standard — 1,499/yr self-serve subscription: the buyer connects (or uploads an export of) their agent run data, gets the binder regenerated on demand and a continuously-updated dashboard. Recurring, low-touch, software-margin. (2) Attested Binder — 6,000–12,000 one-time, repeatable annually: Matt personally reviews the evidence and signs a reviewer attestation, which is what an insurer/auditor actually wants and what justifies the premium. This is the consulting front-door that matches the documented business direction (4–6 engagements/year, proof-of-craft). (3) Multi-entity — 25–40k/yr for firms with several legal entities (the regulatory-compliance plugin's existing Bermuda/Cayman/Channel-Islands specialists make multi-jurisdiction binders a natural expansion). Land with the questionnaire deadline (Standard), expand to Attested when the stakes are a denied claim or a gated contract.
twelve_month_path: Months 1–2: package the existing Sága logs + guard-hook logs + posture YAML into a single "evidence reader" and a binder template; build the three-question intake and the PDF/dashboard generator on the existing pipeline. Months 2–3: map the binder to the three real question sets — one cyber-insurance AI/automation section, one vendor-security questionnaire (SIG/CAIQ-style), and NIST AI RMF + ISO/IEC 42001 control language — with the regulatory-compliance control-testing skill doing the crosswalk. Months 3–4: first design partner — Matt's own consulting clients (the documented Contoso-type live-repo users) facing a real insurer or customer questionnaire; deliver an Attested Binder hands-on, capture every gap as a lesson via the wrap skill. Months 5–8: turn that into the Standard self-serve tier; add a connector to ingest agent activity from where SMBs actually run AI (Microsoft 365 Copilot logs first — the microsoft-365-copilot plugin already exists). Months 9–12: 3–5 Attested engagements booked off inbound "our insurer is asking" demand; one multi-entity reference customer. Honest gating: do NOT build the multi-tenant SaaS backend until at least two Attested engagements prove the binder closes a real deadline.
honest_risks: The evidence is only as strong as what's actually logged. Sága records are rich for RavenClaude's OWN agents; for a customer running Copilot/ChatGPT/Cursor we may only get thin or no logs, so the binder risks documenting our framework rather than their real agent behavior. Mitigation: be explicit in the binder about coverage ('what is monitored vs. attested-by-policy'), and prioritize the M365 Copilot connector — but this gap could make the binder feel hollow to a sharp auditor.

Attestation is a liability surface. The moment Matt signs a reviewer note that an insurer relies on, a missed control or a later incident could expose RavenClaude to a claim. Needs an explicit scope-and-limitations clause and possibly E&O insurance before selling the Attested tier — this is a real cost and a real lawyer conversation, not a footnote.

Questionnaire targets move. Insurer AI sections, SIG/CAIQ, NIST AI RMF, and ISO 42001 all evolve; a stale crosswalk produces wrong answers with false confidence. Requires the knowledge-staleness sweep cadence already in the plugin, and someone owning re-verification — ongoing labor, not a one-time map.

Buyer may not have 'AI agents' yet. Some SMBs feel the questionnaire pressure before they actually run autonomous agents — for them the honest product is lighter (a policy + posture statement), and overselling a 'governance binder' to a shop with two ChatGPT users is a trust risk. Need a qualifying question up front that can route them to the smaller offering.

Crowded adjacency. AI-governance/GRC tooling (Vanta-style, plus AI-specific entrants) is heating up and could add an 'AI agent' module. Our durable edge is the tamper-evident, agent-native log generated from a real running substrate — but that edge erodes if we don't ship the connectors that let it cover the customer's own agents, not just ours.

name: Closebook — the AI financial-close and cash-visibility analyst that hands you an audit trail with every number
pitch: A senior financial analyst that lives in your books, produces your month-end close package and 13-week cash forecast on demand, and attaches a one-click "show your work" trail an auditor or lender will accept — so a 30-person company gets CFO-grade output without a CFO's salary.
buyer: The SMB controller / fractional-CFO / founder-who-does-finance at a 20–150-person company (the exact $25–50k-engagement buyer the synthesis validated in S4). What pays: the recurring pain of month-end close burning 5–10 days, a lender or board asking for a 13-week cash view they can't produce cleanly, and SOC/audit prep that eats a quarter. The trigger purchase is a forcing event on the calendar — fundraise/diligence, a covenant-monitored loan, a SOC 1/SOC 2 cycle, or a board that just started asking harder questions. They pay $20–35k for an initial close-and-forecast build (4–6 weeks), then a $2–5k/mo retainer to run it. This is the SAME wallet the synthesis says is real — I am redirecting it from a horizontal governance promise (which this buyer does not feel viscerally) to a finance deliverable they already lose sleep over.
offering: Concretely they get: (1) a month-end close package — variance commentary, board-pack-ready exhibits — generated from their actuals; (2) a live 13-week direct-method cash forecast with covenant-headroom view and management-action triggers; (3) a driver-based operating forecast and a DCF/valuation view when a transaction is live; (4) a SOC/audit-prep control walkthrough package that satisfies an auditor on first review; and (5) — the differentiator — every figure carries a "show your work" lineage card: source ledger rows, the transformation applied, who/what approved any judgment call, and a timestamp. Delivered as an interactive HTML dashboard (the data-platform embedded-dashboard surface) plus an exportable board PDF. The audit trail is the T1 governance substrate, repurposed: instead of selling "agent governance" abstractly, the immutable lineage shows up as "every number is defensible," which is what a finance buyer actually buys.
why_us: RavenClaude already ships the finance plugin with 7 production agents (controller, treasury-analyst, fpa-analyst, financial-modeler, valuation-analyst, board-pack-composer, audit-prep-specialist — verified at plugins/finance/agents/) and 9 deep skills including thirteen-week-cash-forecast, dcf-valuation, month-end-close, board-pack-composition, variance-commentary, and soc-control-walkthrough (verified at plugins/finance/skills/), backed by 10 knowledge files covering ASC 718/805/740, WACC, accrual/cutoff, and FPA operating models. The data-platform plugin supplies the dashboard, multi-tenant DB, and embedded-JWT surface for delivery. The audit/lineage substrate (Sága-logged decisions, the tribunal verdict log) is the SAME asset T1 leans on — so this isn't a separate build, it's the governance IP wearing a finance jacket. And the founder credibility is unmatched for this exact wedge: Matt IS a financial analyst with Power Platform experience (per memory), and his wife is a Partner Success Manager — the finance + Microsoft-shop SMB profile is his native habitat, not a market he has to learn. The synthesis ranked T4 fourth only because it imagined competing with Microsoft's Finance-Agent-in-Excel; this competes instead as governed *delivery* to companies too small to staff a real FP&A team, where Microsoft's product layer is irrelevant.
simplicity_packaging: The buyer never sees an agent, a tribunal, a YAML posture file, or the word "governance." In the first 5 minutes they connect their accounting source (QuickBooks/Xero/a CSV export) and get back ONE artifact: their actual month-end variance summary or 13-week cash view, on screen, with a "Why this number?" link under each figure that expands the lineage. That single legible deliverable — "here is your close package, and you can defend every line" — is the whole pitch. All the machinery (the seven specialist agents, the FORGE planning loop, the tribunal that gates judgment calls, the immutable event log) is invisible as a pitch but surfaces as the visible trust signal exactly where finance buyers want it: under each number. This resolves the synthesis's C2 contradiction in finance's favor — "invisible machinery, visible evidence" maps perfectly onto a financial deliverable, more naturally than onto an abstract command-governance dashboard.
business_model: Productized consulting, three tiers with an anchored middle: (Bronze, $20k) close-package + cash-forecast build, 4 weeks; (Silver, $32k, the anchor) Bronze + driver-based forecast + audit/lineage package + board PDF, 6 weeks; (Gold, $48k) Silver + SOC/audit-prep walkthrough + valuation view for an active transaction. Then a $2–5k/mo "run-it" retainer to operate the close and forecast each month — recurring revenue that fits solo capacity (the synthesis's C1 caution: 4–6 engagements/year + a stack of retainers is exactly Matt's stated 4–6/year solo model). RavenClaude is the governed accelerator that makes one person deliver CFO-team output; the finance plugin is proof-of-craft, not a self-serve SaaS (the SaaS version is a later bet once the retainer base proves the close engine runs unattended). Pricing sits deliberately inside the validated 25–60k SMB build bracket and 2–8k/mo retainer band from S4.
twelve_month_path: Months 0–2: land 1 design-partner engagement (start with a company in Matt's network — ideally one already on QuickBooks + Microsoft, or via his wife's Partner-Success network where SMB CFO pain is visible); harden the QuickBooks/Xero connector path on the data-platform connector-developer agent; wire the lineage card into the embedded dashboard so every figure links to its source. Months 2–5: deliver the first paid close + 13-week-cash build, capture it as a wrap-skill lesson, and use the real (anonymized) artifact as the sales demo — "show me the live system" beats slideware (S6). Months 5–9: package the three tiers, stand up the retainer for the design partner, close 2–3 more engagements off the demo. Months 9–12: 4–6 builds done, 2–3 retainers running; the audit/lineage substrate is battle-tested on real financials, which de-risks an eventual T1 governance product (this finance wedge is the proving ground for the governance IP, not a detour from it). Decision gate at month 12: if retainers are sticky, invest in self-serve close automation; if delivery stays bespoke, stay consulting-first.
honest_risks: Numbers-are-wrong liability is existential: a finance deliverable that mis-states a close or a covenant calc is far higher-stakes than a governance dashboard being imperfect. The 'show your work' lineage helps defensibility but does NOT remove the need for a human controller to sign off every package — the product must be positioned as analyst-assist, not auto-pilot, and engagement terms must say so.

Accounting-data ingestion is genuinely hard and unglamorous: chart-of-accounts mapping, QuickBooks/Xero API quirks, and messy SMB books will eat more time than the modeling. The connector path is the real cost center, and the synthesis never priced it.

Solo capacity is the binding constraint (synthesis C1): finance delivery is high-touch and hard to make low-touch fast, so revenue is capped by Matt's hours until the close engine demonstrably runs unattended — the retainer thesis is unproven.

Microsoft/incumbent encroachment is real even if I argued it's overstated: Finance-Agent-in-Excel and the Vena/Acterys layer could move down-market, and if QuickBooks ships a 'close + audit trail' agent, the wedge narrows. The moat is the integrated lineage + senior-judgment quality, which is defensible but not permanent.

Trust/sales-cycle friction: a CFO/controller is a skeptical, conservative buyer who will not hand financials to an AI on a first call. The sales motion needs a referenceable real engagement and likely a human-in-the-loop guarantee, lengthening time-to-first-dollar.

This bets against the synthesis's own #1 ranking. If the agent-governance window (T1, est. 12–18 months) is closing faster than finance demand converts, the opportunity cost is real — though the strongest counter is that this finance play USES and battle-tests the T1 substrate rather than abandoning it, so the bet is hedged, not exclusive.

name: The Compounding Engagement: RavenClaude Forge
pitch: Matt runs a fixed-scope consulting engagement that ships a working AI-operations system into the client's business — and every engagement quietly deposits the reusable parts back into a growing library, so each project is faster, cheaper to deliver, and worth more than the last.
buyer: A mid-market EdTech, Power Platform, or finance-ops leader (Director of Ops / RevOps / Partner Success — explicitly NOT IT) who has a painful, repetitive, judgment-heavy workflow (bulk flow buildouts, partner-health reporting, support-ticket triage, regulatory checks) and an executive mandate to "use AI" but no safe way to do it. The purchase trigger is a board/exec directive plus a concrete backlog they can't staff. They pay $25–50k for a fixed 4–6 week engagement (Matt's validated price), with an optional $1.5–3k/mo "keep-it-current" retainer afterward. First customer: the existing ~136-cloud-flow Power Platform DEV client already in flight — turn that live delivery into the reference engagement and first Forge.
offering: The client gets a named, installed "Forge" — a private bundle of RavenClaude plugins (e.g. power-platform + data-platform + the relevant domain plugin) configured to THEIR environment, plus: (1) the actual delivered work product (the 136 flows, the partner-health dashboard, the triage pipeline) running in their tenant; (2) a one-page plain-language "Operating Brief" of what was built and how to re-run it; (3) the comfort-posture + tribunal guardrails pre-set so their non-developer staff can safely re-invoke the agents without breaking anything. What they never see: the 24-plugin marketplace, the FORGE planning pipeline, worktrees, CI gates, or the tribunal engine. They see a working thing and a "Run it again" button.
why_us: The consulting-to-product flywheel is already BUILT and verified in-repo, not aspirational: the `/wrap` command (plugins/ravenclaude-core/commands/wrap.md) captures an engagement lesson as structured YAML+markdown, the `contribute-finding` and `review-staged-contributions` skills (plugins/ravenclaude-core/skills/) route it through docs/staging/incoming/ into a plugin, and the FORGE pipeline (skills/forge-pipeline) turns any client idea into a reviewed, tribunal-checked plan. That is the literal mechanism by which each engagement compounds into reusable IP. Credibility assets that already exist: 24 plugins (ravenclaude-core at v0.122.0), real domain depth (power-platform: 11 agents/18 skills; data-platform: 4 agents/13 skills; edtech-partner-success: 6/16), the multi-layer comfort-posture safety system, and the tribunal/decision-review guardrails that make handing agents to non-developers defensible. No generic "AI consultant" has a versioned, CI-gated, self-improving methodology library to install.
simplicity_packaging: The buyer's first 5 minutes: a single "Your Forge" dashboard (one URL) with three tiles — "What we built," "Run it again," and "What's safe to change." No commands to memorize (honoring Matt's dashboards-over-slash-commands constraint). Under the hood it's the full marketplace, FORGE planning, worktrees, and tribunal review — but the client only ever sees plain-language outcomes and a big safe button. The complexity is hidden behind the comfort-posture defaults (set conservative for non-developers) and the Operating Brief written in their vocabulary, not engineering terms.
business_model: Productized fixed-scope consulting: $25–50k per Forge engagement, 4–6/year solo (Matt's validated capacity and price). Margin compounds because the marketplace does the heavy lifting and each `/wrap`-captured lesson cuts delivery time on the next similar engagement. Recurring layer: $1.5–3k/mo "Forge Care" retainer (knowledge-staleness sweeps, posture tuning, re-runs) — turns one-time projects into a renewing base. Long-bet optionality: once 3–4 Forges exist in the same vertical (e.g. EdTech Partner Success), the shared plugin becomes a productizable managed offering — but that's funded by consulting cash, never the entry bet. The website is the front door: positioning + "Book a call," with delivered Forges as the proof gallery.
twelve_month_path: Months 1–2: convert the in-flight 136-flow Power Platform client into Engagement #0 — deliver, then run `/wrap` to deposit the first reusable lessons; build the single "Your Forge" client dashboard (thin wrapper over existing dashboard infra). Months 3–4: ship the consulting website (positioning + booking + Forge proof gallery) and run Engagement #1 in a second vertical (EdTech Partner Success — plugin already mature at 6 agents/16 skills) to prove cross-domain repeatability. Months 5–8: reach 3 completed Forges; formalize Forge Care retainer; measure delivery-time reduction engagement-over-engagement to validate the compounding claim with numbers. Months 9–12: 4–6 engagements landed at target price, 2+ retainers active; pick the vertical with the most `/wrap` deposits as the first candidate for a productized/managed offering (the SaaS long bet), but only scope it — don't build it yet.
honest_risks: Solo-founder capacity is the hard ceiling: 4–6 engagements/year leaves almost no slack for sales, website, AND a SaaS build — the compounding only pays off if Matt protects delivery time and resists scope creep into product too early.

The compounding claim must be MEASURED, not asserted. If engagement #2 isn't demonstrably faster/cheaper than #1, the whole 'system connecting consulting to product' thesis is just normal consulting with good tooling. Instrument delivery hours from day one.

Handing self-improving agents to non-developers is a genuine liability surface — a tribunal/posture misconfiguration that lets a client's staff break their own production tenant is reputationally fatal. The guardrails exist but have only been battle-tested in Matt's own repo, not in hostile client environments.

IP/confidentiality boundary: the `/wrap`-to-marketplace flow could accidentally carry a client's proprietary logic into a reusable plugin. Needs an explicit scrubbing/consent step in contribute-finding before this is contractually safe — currently a behavioral norm, not an enforced gate.

Market education cost is high: 'productized AI-ops consulting with a compounding methodology library' is not a category buyers search for. Early sales will be relationship-driven (the existing client, referrals), and the abstract pitch may not convert cold — the proof gallery has to do the talking.

Anthropic/platform dependency: the entire methodology is bound to Claude Code's plugin and agent mechanics. A breaking change to the plugin/marketplace model or a pricing shift could force a costly re-platform of the core asset.

name: Leash — the safety inspection for the AI you let touch your business
pitch: Leash is a "safety sticker" for AI agents: it watches every action your AI assistant tries to take, blocks the dangerous ones before they happen, and hands you a one-page receipt proving your AI is on a leash you can see — the way a fire-marshal certificate proves a building is safe to occupy.
buyer: WHO PAYS: the owner/operator of a small business or boutique consultancy (5–50 people) that has started letting AI agents touch real systems — code, customer data, financial records, cloud accounts. Not a developer; an analyst, ops lead, or founder. TRIGGER (the forcing function, per synthesis §S3/C3/§4): not regulation — it's the annual insurance renewal and the vendor security questionnaire. A Tech E&O underwriter (ISO filed three GenAI exclusions effective Jan 2026; Chubb/Travelers/CNA added AI exclusions — adversa.ai, traverselegal.com) or a larger customer's procurement desk now asks verbatim "Do you use AI? Do you police it? Show me the audit trail" (modulos.ai, traverselegal.com). Leash is what they buy the week that question lands and they realize they have no answer. WHAT THEY PAY: $99/mo solo "Leashed" tier (self-serve, one workstation); $499/mo "Leashed Team" (up to 10 seats, shared posture, exportable evidence pack); and a one-time $7,500 "Leash Inspection" — a fixed-scope engagement that installs it, sets the posture with them, and hands over the first signed evidence pack for their renewal. The inspection is the on-ramp that funnels into the recurring subscription, and sits comfortably under the validated $25–50k boutique bracket (synthesis §S4).
offering: Concretely, the buyer gets: (1) A LEASH STATUS LIGHT — a single dashboard tile that is green/yellow/red, answering "is my AI on a leash right now?" with no config to read. (2) THREE PRESET LEASHES — Strict / Balanced / Exploratory — chosen from a dropdown, never authored by hand (maps to the existing comfort-posture.yaml presets). (3) THE STOP — when the AI tries something dangerous (delete files, push to production, touch secrets, spend money), it is blocked BEFORE it happens and the buyer sees plain-English "I stopped your AI from X because Y." (4) THE RECEIPT — a one-page, timestamped "Leash Certificate" export: what the AI did, what was blocked, who approved what, generated live from the immutable decision log — the artifact you attach to an insurance renewal or hand a procurement reviewer. (5) THE BIG RED BUTTON — a one-click kill switch that freezes all agent activity. Under the hood these are RavenClaude's tribunal (the binding ALLOW/EDIT/DENY panel), comfort-posture, the Sága-logged decision substrate, and Ragnarök — but the buyer never sees those words.
why_us: This is the rare case where the differentiator already physically exists on disk, verified this session: (a) BINDING IN-LOOP TRIBUNAL — `.ravenclaude/runs/thing/decisions/decide-*.json` shows real timestamped ALLOW/EDIT/DENY verdicts on live commands; the synthesis (§S1) found NO competitor combines multi-agent deliberation into a binding verdict on a live action — LLM juries exist only as offline scoring, governance platforms gate with single-engine deterministic rules. (b) THE FOUR GOVERNANCE PRIMITIVES MAP 1:1 (§S3, querypie taxonomy): Permission=comfort-posture.yaml (schema v5, presets verified), Approval=the Thing, Audit Trail=the Sága decision log, Kill Switch=ragnarok/reset-plugin-cache — all present in the repo. (c) THE NON-DEVELOPER SURFACE is the unoccupied seat (§S2): every competitor (agentic-os, Aperion Shield, MCP Governance) is dev-centric OSS with no non-developer dashboard, and `dashboard.html` + `serve-dashboards.py` already render the surface. (d) "the Thing cannot disable itself" hardening (PR #94, T4) and the egress secret backstop mean the leash can't be quietly unclipped — a real trust property, not a checkbox. No one else can credibly ship this in 2026 because the binding-verdict engine took a year of tribunal work (T0–T4) to harden.
simplicity_packaging: The machinery is hidden behind ONE question the buyer actually has: "is my AI going to do something I'll regret, and can I prove to my insurer/customer that it won't?" First 5 minutes: install the watcher, pick a preset leash from a dropdown, then deliberately ask your AI to do something reckless (e.g., "delete my whole project") — and WATCH IT GET STOPPED, with a plain-English reason. That single demonstrated "Stop" is the aha (synthesis §S6: engineer a first-run aha that produces one real artifact, not a tutorial). The words tribunal, posture, FORGE, Sága, JSONL never appear in the UI. Per the C2 resolution — "invisible machinery, visible evidence" — we hide the mechanism but surface the artifact: the Leash Certificate is sitting on the dashboard within the first session, downloadable, with the buyer's name and date on it. Complex underneath (a hardened multi-seat deliberation engine); on the surface it's a green light, a dropdown, a stop sign, a receipt, and a red button.
business_model: Land-and-expand productized service → SaaS, exactly the T1-as-IP / T2-as-distribution resolution (synthesis §C1). LAND: the $7,500 fixed-scope "Leash Inspection" — solo-deliverable in days, funded by the insurance/procurement trigger, lands one real evidence pack. EXPAND: convert to recurring $99/$499 per month subscription for the live status light + ongoing certificate generation (the renewal is annual, so the evidence need recurs). The subscription is deliberately low-touch/self-serve so revenue isn't capped by Matt's hours (the §C1 warning). Year-1 economics for a solo operator: ~12 inspections ($90k) + a subscription base growing to ~40 paying seats blended ($120k ARR run-rate) is a realistic solo ceiling; a second person or a partner unlocks the Team tier as the scale lever. The product (Leash) is the moat; the inspection is the distribution.
twelve_month_path: Months 0–2: package the existing tribunal+posture+log+kill-switch into a single installable "Leash" surface; build the Leash Certificate exporter (one HTML→PDF page off the decision log) and the green/yellow/red status tile; write the three preset leashes as named bundles. Months 2–4: run 2–3 free "Leash Inspections" for friendly small shops (Matt's network — the Contoso consumer repo is a live testbed) to validate the install + certificate against a REAL insurance questionnaire; collect the exact wording underwriters use. Months 4–7: charge for the inspection; stand up self-serve billing (Stripe) and the $99/$499 subscription tiers; harden the cross-platform bridge so the leash follows the user across Claude Code AND Copilot CLI (§S7). Months 7–12: pursue an insurance-broker or MSP referral partner who hears the "show me your AI controls" question all day and can route deals; map the certificate fields to NIST AI RMF / ISO 42001 clause language (§T3) so it survives a sophisticated reviewer. Gate to next year: ≥10 paying subscriptions and ≥1 certificate that actually closed a renewal or a vendor questionnaire.
honest_risks: Window is 12–18 months (synthesis §C4): an identity/observability incumbent (Galileo, a Microsoft toolkit, or an eval vendor) could repackage a binding-verdict mode in one product decision. The moat is the integrated non-developer experience + audit substrate, not any single mechanism — and that's a thinner moat than it feels.

The binding tribunal runs via `claude -p` seats — it needs live model calls, adds latency to every gated action, and costs tokens per decision. For a $99/mo tier the per-decision economics must be proven; an always-on multi-seat panel on every action may be too slow or too expensive and may need a cheaper deterministic fast-path with the panel reserved for genuinely risky actions.

The Leash Certificate is NOT a recognized compliance artifact — no accredited body stands behind it (the §T5 catch: certification revenue accrues to Schellman/BSI, not the prep tool). It must be honestly sold as 'evidence you can show,' never as 'certified/compliant,' or an analytically-minded buyer (and Matt is exactly that buyer) will rightly call it theater.

Trigger dependency: the whole urgency rests on insurers and procurement desks asking the question. If that pressure softens, or if Anthropic/Microsoft ship a built-in governance panel into the base CLI, the standalone product's reason-to-buy erodes fast.

Solo capacity: the inspection on-ramp is human-delivered and caps growth at Matt's hours until the self-serve subscription motion genuinely works without him — the §C1 build-vs-deliver tension is a real fork, not a footnote.

Load-bearing demand stats in the synthesis (34%-more-confident-when-wrong, 47%/22% monitoring figures) are flagged unverified secondary aggregators (§C5) — they must NOT appear in buyer-facing material without primary-source verification, per Matt's fact-check discipline.
